cataloging_subject.label: Troilus and Criseyde
cataloging_subject.scope_note: Geoffrey Chaucer's five-book Middle English poem, as represented in CME00002 by Frederick J. Furnivall's parallel transcriptions of the Campsall, Harley 2280, and Cambridge Gg.4.27 witnesses. The work is distinct from those three manuscript objects, Furnivall's 1881–1882 edition, its 1967 Johnson reprint, the U-M electronic encoding, and this repository's XML artifact. The XML serializes three witness versions within each book; those versions are representations of one work, not fifteen intrinsic chapters.

catalog_summary.title: Troilus and Criseyde
catalog_summary.author: Geoffrey Chaucer
catalog_summary.region: England; exact place of composition not securely established; narrative set in Troy

titles.value: A Parallel-Text Print of Chaucer's Troilus and Criseyde from the Campsall MS. of Mr. Bacon Frank, the Harleian MS. 2280, and Cambridge University Library MS. Gg.4.27
notes: A shortened transcription of the edition's long title. It names the parallel-text source layer rather than a medieval authorial title.
Used as the edition's opening/running heading; the XML miskeys one occurrence as ‘Chancer's Troilus.’

scope.description: Creative retelling drawing especially on Giovanni Boccaccio's Il Filostrato
The 1881–1882 parallel-text source edition represented by CME00002
Parallel diplomatic transcriptions of the Campsall, Harley 2280, and Gg.4.27 witnesses
Alignment, lineation, witness notes, and source-comparison marks in the parallel-text edition

assertion.notes: The Middle English Compendium's stencil gives c1385; the approximation is not normalized to an exact year.

scope.description: The earliest securely datable complete witness

assertion.notes: This dates an extant copy, not composition.

notes: This does not assign a precise composition place or any one witness's scribal dialect to the abstract poem.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: Brief Latin incipits, explicits, names, and learned quotations do not make the poem as cataloged a substantively multilingual work.

form_statements.description: A five-book narrative poem predominantly in seven-line rhyme-royal stanzas (ababbcc), with embedded songs, letters, and prayers retained as verse.

summaries.value: A five-book Middle English narrative poem recounting Troilus's love for Criseyde during the Trojan War, their union through Pandarus, Criseyde's exchange to the Greek camp and relationship with Diomede, and Troilus's death and final vision beyond earthly mutability. Chaucer transforms Boccaccio's Il Filostrato through sustained reflection on love, agency, fidelity, Fortune, providence, and authorship.

CME00002 represents Furnivall's three-column edition as sequential XML divisions: for each of the five books, Campsall is version 1, Harley 2280 version 2, and Gg.4.27 version 3. Gg.4.27 is materially incomplete, and Harley 2280 lacks one leaf in Book V. Counts below therefore describe the encoding's witness streams, not one canonical text.

extent.description: Five intrinsic books represented through fifteen witness-version divisions. The repository XML contains 3,410 LG elements and 23,862 L elements across all three transcriptions: Campsall 1,176/8,237; Harley 1,164/8,154; Gg.4.27 1,070/7,471. These are encoding metrics, not the stanza or line count of a single critical text.

content_parts.label: Book I
content_parts.summary: Troilus first sees and falls in love with Criseyde; Pandarus undertakes to mediate.
notes: Metrics aggregate the three witness versions; Gg.4.27 lacks its opening two leaves and later leaves 28–29.

content_parts.label: Book II
content_parts.summary: Pandarus advances the courtship and brings Troilus and Criseyde into communication.
notes: Metrics aggregate the three witness versions; Gg.4.27's opening is interrupted by removed leaves.

content_parts.label: Book III
content_parts.summary: The lovers are united and experience the poem's central period of joy.
notes: Metrics aggregate the three witness versions; Gg.4.27 loses a leaf at the book boundary.

content_parts.label: Book IV
content_parts.summary: Criseyde is exchanged for Antenor, separating the lovers and provoking debate over destiny and free will.
notes: Metrics aggregate the three witness versions; Gg.4.27 has major material and textual omissions in this book.

content_parts.label: Book V
content_parts.summary: Criseyde remains with the Greeks and Diomede; Troilus dies in battle and looks back on earthly love from the heavens.
notes: Metrics aggregate the three witness versions. Harley 2280 loses one leaf (Book V, lines 1345–1428), and Gg.4.27 loses its ending with leaves 127–131.

related_works.title: Il Filostrato
notes: Boccaccio is Chaucer's principal narrative source, but the poem is a substantial creative transformation rather than a line-for-line translation. Furnivall's side marks quantify degrees of resemblance using William Michael Rossetti's comparison.

evidence.locator: Two title-page divisions; Books I–V; fifteen DIV3 witness versions; opening and closing notes
evidence.summary: Identifies CME00002, Furnivall's parallel-text title, the 1967 Johnson reprint statement, the five-book structure, three witness streams, printed-page and manuscript-leaf milestones, lacunae, and the encoded poem.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, ‘A parallel-text print of Chaucer's Troilus and Criseyde,’ CME item CME00002.
evidence.summary: Confirms the item title, Chaucer attribution, London/Chaucer Society 1881–1882 imprint, public full text, stable identifier, and No Copyright–United States statement.

evidence.citation: Middle English Compendium, Bibliography entry BIB625, ‘Troilus and Criseyde.’
evidence.locator: Entry Info; manuscript list; Furnivall edition; witness stencils
evidence.summary: Supplies IMEV/NIMEV/DIMEV identifiers, the 1881–1882 two-part Furnivall citation, the three source witnesses and modern shelfmarks, c1385 work date in the MED stencil, and witness-specific dates/localizations without conflating them with the work.

evidence.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., A Parallel-Text Print of Chaucer's Troilus and Criseyde, Part I (Chaucer Society, First Series 63, 1881), Google Books volume 6Ue_hzUjTUEC.
evidence.summary: Visible scan confirms the edition title, Furnivall's responsibility, three parallel columns, Boccaccio/Rossetti side-mark legend, witness headings, manuscript omissions, and printed layout for Books I–II.

evidence.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., A One-Text Print of Chaucer's Troilus and Criseyde from the Campsall MS. (London: Chaucer Society, 1888), Internet Archive item aonetextprintch00furngoog.
evidence.locator: Title page; Forewords, pp. vii–viii; complete Campsall text
evidence.summary: Furnivall explains that he initially selected and typeset Campsall as a prospective basis text, marked Boccaccio parallels from Rossetti, later judged Harley 2280 the better manuscript, and nevertheless issued the already-set Campsall text.

evidence.citation: Morgan Library & Museum, MS M.817, Troilus and Criseyde.
evidence.summary: Catalogues the 120-leaf parchment Campsall witness, probably made in London in 1403–1413, and describes its royal provenance, decoration, script, and current shelfmark.

evidence.citation: Broadview Press, Troilus and Criseyde, ed. James M. Dean and Harriet Spiegel.
evidence.summary: Describes the work as a Middle English narrative of a tragic love affair, an archetypal medieval romance set in Troy, and identifies Boccaccio and Benoît among Chaucer's sources.

evidence.citation: University of Cambridge Faculty of English, ‘Form,’ Troilus & Criseyde: Translation & Commentary.
evidence.summary: Defines the poem's seven-line ababbcc rhyme-royal stanza and pentameter basis.

evidence.citation: Structural count of General Corpus CME00002.xml using lxml element paths.
evidence.summary: Counts five book divisions, fifteen witness-version divisions, 3,410 LG and 23,862 L elements; per witness: Campsall 1,176/8,237, Harley 1,164/8,154, and Gg.4.27 1,070/7,471.

evidence.citation: General Corpus lineage manifest for CME00002.
evidence.summary: Binds descriptive claims to the repository artifact, U-M encoding, Johnson reprint, Furnivall edition, and three manuscript witnesses.

open_questions.question: Can the composition and revision chronology be narrowed beyond the conventional c. 1385 dating without overstating disputed internal and historical evidence?
next_steps: Consult a current critical introduction devoted to the poem's dating and revision phases before adding normalized bounds.

open_questions.question: Which linguistic features belong to Chaucer's compositional language and which belong to the three scribes or Furnivall's transcription conventions?
next_steps: Use reading-level manuscript and editorial analysis; do not assign the work one source witness's LALME localization.

notes: The work record deliberately catalogs one poem, while the lineage record preserves three independent witness streams and their material gaps.
Furnivall's printed page arrangement is parallel; the XML's sequential DIV3 structure is an encoding transformation and must not be interpreted as the poem's intrinsic order.
No single dialect region is assigned to the abstract poem from this multi-witness source.
